working-tree-staging-and-history.label: Move a Change into History
working-tree-staging-and-history.summary: Follow one edit from the working tree to the staging area and into a named commit, checking each boundary along the way.

sketch0.title: The Working Tree Is Your Draft

try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.title: Read a Working-Tree Change
try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.prompt:
You are working in the prepared `study-notes/` repository.

A committed `notes.txt` has a new line that is not staged. Use status and diff to inspect the working-tree change.
try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.hint: Inspect the working-tree patch before changing the staging area.

help.concept: The working tree is where edits live before they are selected for a commit.
help.hint_1: Focus on the removed and added lines for the path named in the task.
help.hint_2: The patch should contain only the edit described in the scenario.

try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.starterCode:
Git tracks snapshots.
The working tree can change first.
try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.instructions:
You are working in the prepared `study-notes/` repository.

A committed `notes.txt` has a new line that is not staged. Use status and diff to inspect the working-tree change.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf study-notes
mkdir -p study-notes
git -C study-notes init -q -b main
git -C study-notes config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C study-notes config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd study-notes
printf %s 'Git tracks snapshots.
' > notes.txt
cd ..
git -C study-notes add -A
git -C study-notes commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd study-notes
printf %s 'Git tracks snapshots.
The working tree can change first.
' > notes.txt
cd ..

printf %s 'Git tracks snapshots.
' > notes.txt
cd ..
git -C study-notes add -A
git -C study-notes commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd study-notes
printf %s 'Git tracks snapshots.
The working tree can change first.
' > notes.txt
cd ..

try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.title: Move the Change into the Staging Area
try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.prompt:
You are working in the prepared `study-notes/` repository.

Stage only `notes.txt`, then inspect the staged file name and staged diff.
try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.hint: Choose only the paths that belong to this checkpoint.

help.concept: git add copies the chosen working-tree content into the staging area; it does not create history yet.
help.hint_1: Do not broaden the staging boundary just to make status quieter.
help.hint_2: Review the staged paths or patch before moving on.

try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.starterCode:
Git tracks snapshots.
The staging area selects the next snapshot.
try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.solutionCode:
git add notes.txt
git diff --staged --name-only
git diff --staged -- notes.txt
try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.instructions:
You are working in the prepared `study-notes/` repository.

Stage only `notes.txt`, then inspect the staged file name and staged diff.

try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.title: Record the Staged Snapshot
try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.prompt:
You are working in the prepared `study-notes/` repository.

The notes change is already staged. Commit it with `Explain the staging area`, then read the latest one-line history entry.
try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.hint: Record only the staged snapshot and use the message requested by the scenario.

help.concept: git commit records the staged snapshot as a new history entry; unstaged work is not included.
help.hint_1: The message should describe the project result, not the commands you used.
help.hint_2: After recording it, inspect history and any work that remains.

try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.starterCode:
Git tracks snapshots.
The staging area selects the next snapshot.
try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.solutionCode:
git commit -m "Explain the staging area"
git log -1 --oneline
try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.instructions:
You are working in the prepared `study-notes/` repository.

The notes change is already staged. Commit it with `Explain the staging area`, then read the latest one-line history entry.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf study-notes
mkdir -p study-notes
git -C study-notes init -q -b main
git -C study-notes config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C study-notes config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd study-notes
printf %s 'Git tracks snapshots.
' > notes.txt
cd ..
git -C study-notes add -A
git -C study-notes commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd study-notes
printf %s 'Git tracks snapshots.
The staging area selects the next snapshot.
' > notes.txt
cd ..
git -C study-notes add -- notes.txt

try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.title: Verify the Three Areas Agree
try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.prompt:
You are working in the prepared `study-notes/` repository.

The second notes snapshot is complete. Use short status and one-line log to verify a clean working tree and two commits.
try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.hint: Read the timeline from newest checkpoint to oldest.

help.concept: After a successful commit, the working tree and index can match HEAD again while the history keeps both snapshots.
help.hint_1: Focus on commit order and subject lines rather than machine-specific hashes.
help.hint_2: The newest entry should describe the result from this milestone.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf study-notes
mkdir -p study-notes
git -C study-notes init -q -b main
git -C study-notes config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C study-notes config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd study-notes
printf %s 'Git tracks snapshots.
' > notes.txt
cd ..
git -C study-notes add -A
git -C study-notes commit -q -m 'Start study notes'
cd study-notes
printf %s 'Git tracks snapshots.
The staging area selects the next snapshot.
' > notes.txt
cd ..
git -C study-notes add -A
git -C study-notes commit -q -m 'Explain the staging area'

single-choice-1.title: Identify the Working Tree
single-choice-1.prompt: What is the working tree in Git?
single-choice-1.hint: Focus on what the command or concept observes or changes at this point in the workflow.

help.concept: Follow one edit from the working tree to the staging area and into a named commit, checking each boundary along the way.
help.hint_1: Remove options that act on a different repository layer than the one named in the question.
help.hint_2: Choose the answer that remains accurate even when other unrelated files exist.

options.a: The directory where you make changes to files
options.b: The area where changes are staged for commit

single-choice-2.title: Purpose of the Staging Area
single-choice-2.prompt: What is the purpose of the staging area in Git?
single-choice-2.hint: Focus on what the command or concept observes or changes at this point in the workflow.

help.concept: Follow one edit from the working tree to the staging area and into a named commit, checking each boundary along the way.
help.hint_1: Remove options that act on a different repository layer than the one named in the question.
help.hint_2: Choose the answer that remains accurate even when other unrelated files exist.

options.a: To prepare changes for the next commit

multi-choice-1.title: Components of a Git Repository
multi-choice-1.prompt: Which of the following are components of a Git repository? Select all that apply.
multi-choice-1.hint: Judge each option independently; choose only the statements that stay true in this exact repository state.

help.concept: Follow one edit from the working tree to the staging area and into a named commit, checking each boundary along the way.
help.hint_1: A partly true option is still wrong if it adds an effect Git does not guarantee.
help.hint_2: Use the layer named in the prompt—working tree, staging area, or history—to test each choice.

drag-reorder-1.title: Order of Git Workflow
drag-reorder-1.prompt: Arrange the steps of the Git workflow in the correct order.
drag-reorder-1.hint: Start with the state that must exist before the next action can make sense.

help.concept: Follow one edit from the working tree to the staging area and into a named commit, checking each boundary along the way.
help.hint_1: Track how the project moves from working files to a proposed snapshot and then to history.
help.hint_2: Read the ordered steps as one continuous repository story.

tokens.t1: Edit files in the working tree
tokens.t2: Stage changes in the staging area
tokens.t3: Commit changes to the commit history

fill-blank-choice-1.title: Command to Stage Files
fill-blank-choice-1.prompt: Fill in the blank to stage a file for commit: `git [blank1] filename.txt`.
fill-blank-choice-1.hint: Choose the term or symbol that matches the exact state described, not a nearby Git concept.

help.concept: Follow one edit from the working tree to the staging area and into a named commit, checking each boundary along the way.
help.hint_1: Pay attention to whether the prompt describes an untracked path, a working-tree edit, staged work, or history.
help.hint_2: Read spaces, punctuation, and status columns as meaningful when the question shows Git output.

fill-blank-choice-2.title: Command to View Commit History

fill-blank-choice-2.hint: Choose the term or symbol that matches the exact state described, not a nearby Git concept.

working-tree-intro.title: The Working Tree Is Your Draft
working-tree-intro.bodyMarkdown:
The working tree is the project you can open and edit now. When a tracked file changes, the working tree moves ahead of the latest commit, but history stays untouched.

Imagine `hours.txt` gains the line `Open Saturdays`. Short status reports a working-tree modification, and a plain diff shows the exact line. At this stage the change is still a draft: you may refine it, discard it, or decide it belongs in a future snapshot.

This freedom is why Git does not commit every edit automatically.

Suppose `hours.txt` was edited after the last commit. Inspect the file-level state, then the line-level change:

```bash
git status --short
git diff -- hours.txt
```

Status identifies the changed path. Diff shows the exact unstaged lines, so you can review the draft before selecting it for history.

staging-area-intro.title: The Staging Area Is a Proposal
staging-area-intro.bodyMarkdown:
The staging area holds the version you are proposing for the next commit. Staging `hours.txt` copies its current contents into that proposal.

The working file remains editable. If you change it again after staging, Git can show two versions at once: the staged version selected for the commit and a newer unstaged draft.

Think of staging as editorial selection, not permanent storage. It lets you shape a focused checkpoint before history changes.

After the edit is ready, stage that file and inspect the proposal:

```bash
git add hours.txt
git diff --staged --name-only
git diff --staged -- hours.txt
```

The name-only view checks the boundary; the second diff checks the content. Neither command creates a commit.

commit-history-intro.title: A Commit Records the Proposal
commit-history-intro.bodyMarkdown:
A commit records the staged snapshot with an author, a message, and a link to the previous checkpoint. Only staged content enters it. Untracked files and unstaged edits stay outside.

A message such as `Add Saturday hours` explains what became true in the project. Messages such as `changes` or `work` force future readers to open the commit just to learn its purpose.

A focused commit therefore has two qualities: a coherent set of files and a message that names the result.

Once the staged snapshot is correct, record it and read the newest timeline entry:

```bash
git commit -m "Add Saturday hours"
git log -1 --oneline
```

`-m` supplies the commit subject. `-1` asks the log for only the newest checkpoint, which should carry that subject.

git-status-bridge.title: Close the Loop with Evidence
git-status-bridge.bodyMarkdown:
After committing, read the one-line history and then check status. The history should show the new message at the top. A quiet short-status result should show that no work remains outside the commit.

These two views answer different questions: history proves what was recorded; status proves what is still unfinished.

Together they turn “I think I committed it” into a verifiable conclusion.

Close the loop with history and state:

```bash
git log --oneline
git status --short
```

The log proves the snapshot exists. A quiet short status proves the working tree and staging area now match it.
